nodes.label: Artificial intelligence
nodes.rationale: Track covers using AI in daily work, machine learning, deep learning, and LLMs — from practical prompting through how models work.

nodes.label: AI Applied
nodes.rationale: For everyone who uses ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, Copilot — prompting, agents, tools, skills, custom assistants, trust; start here if you mainly interact with products, not train models.

nodes.label: AI Applied (practical AI)
nodes.rationale: Practical AI for people who use ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, Cursor — not for training models or reading research papers. Goal is better outputs, workflows, and trust.

nodes.label: Loop prompting
nodes.rationale: Store instructions once, then send short deltas in the same session or on a schedule — not re-prompt every time.

nodes.label: User mental model (prompt + context → AI → answer)
nodes.rationale: You control goal, tone, format, examples, files/context, when to stop; AI controls wording, reasoning, which tool to call, step order.
nodes.norm_label: user mental model (prompt + context → ai → answer)

nodes.label: 2024–2026 shift: chat to loops and agents
nodes.rationale: Era progression: Chat → Loop prompting → Assistants → Agents → Orchestration; products expose this in UI — need clear goals, context, verification.
nodes.norm_label: 2024–2026 shift: chat to loops and agents

nodes.label: AI agent
nodes.rationale: A model that pursues a goal over multiple steps — planning, calling tools, adjusting on failure — instead of answering in one shot. Users direct agents in products rather than deploying them.

nodes.label: Assistant mode
nodes.rationale: Question plus saved docs/instructions; reply grounded in your knowledge.

nodes.label: Agent mode
nodes.rationale: You give a goal; AI plans → acts → observes → repeats until done or blocked.

nodes.label: Agentic orchestration
nodes.rationale: Coordinating steps and tools to complete a workflow; user designs goals and guardrails, product runs the loop.

nodes.label: Tools (LLM tool calling)
nodes.rationale: Actions the model can request through the host — search, files, code, APIs — instead of only generating text. Host registers tool names/descriptions; LLM picks; host runs; result back to LLM.

nodes.label: App connectors (OAuth)
nodes.rationale: Let the host read or act in SaaS; watch OAuth scopes — wrong scope means over-broad access.

nodes.label: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
nodes.rationale: Adds custom tools via connector programs; wire format is JSON-RPC (stdio or HTTP).

nodes.label: Skill (SKILL.md)
nodes.rationale: A skill is not a tool — loaded as context/playbook. Skills trade a small fixed instruction cost for less chat thrash.

nodes.label: Script as tool
nodes.rationale: A script can be a tool when the host can run it (terminal, MCP, Custom Action). Scripts trade one tool round for not asking the model to simulate an API.

nodes.label: Directing agents
nodes.rationale: Add clear done state, boundaries, tools allowed, checkpoints, and verification on top of effective prompting building blocks.

nodes.label: Human-in-the-loop
nodes.rationale: Treat agent output as draft: Plan → approve → execute → review → ship. For legal/medical/financial/production, you are accountable.

nodes.label: Custom assistants & knowledge
nodes.rationale: Attach your documents and standing instructions so AI answers like a teammate who read the handbook — without fine-tuning models yourself.

nodes.label: Building a useful custom assistant
nodes.rationale: Same idea everywhere: instructions + knowledge + optional tools. Purpose, audience, tone, boundaries, few-shot Q&As, knowledge files, edge-case tests.

nodes.label: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
nodes.rationale: AI searches your files and writes using those chunks; product chunks, embeds, searches on each question and injects passages into the prompt.

nodes.label: Team knowledge libraries
nodes.rationale: Governance: owner per assistant, changelog when policies update.

nodes.label: Memory features
nodes.rationale: Products remember facts across chats; wrong memory persists — correct or delete; turn off for shared machines or sensitive work.

nodes.label: Effective prompting
nodes.rationale: You are not programming the model — you are specifying the task so the model has enough context to help once, not after five retries. Promote working prompts to persistent instructions via loop prompting.

nodes.label: Minimum good prompt
nodes.rationale: Four pieces when the task matters: Role/perspective, Task, Constraints, Output format.

nodes.label: Chain-of-thought
nodes.rationale: Ask for reasoning before the final answer when mistakes are costly; hide steps in copy if you only need the conclusion.

nodes.label: Prompt iteration loop
nodes.rationale: Rough prompt → fix ambiguity → add one constraint or example per iteration → save the prompt that worked. New chat when topic changes; continue when refining same deliverable.

nodes.label: System / project instructions
nodes.rationale: Put stable rules in system/project instructions; put this task's content in the user message.

nodes.label: MCP (Model Context Protocol)
nodes.rationale: How Cursor, Claude Desktop, and Claude Code plug into external systems through MCP servers; configure once, agent calls tools the server exposes.

nodes.label: JSON-RPC 2.0
nodes.rationale: MCP is not gRPC. JSON-RPC is a thin envelope for named remote methods with JSON params/results; picked because simple, human-readable, works over stdio pipes.

nodes.label: stdio transport
nodes.rationale: Local — host spawns server as subprocess; JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout. Most common in IDEs.

nodes.label: Streamable HTTP transport
nodes.rationale: Remote web service MCP; POST JSON-RPC, optional SSE. Prefer over older HTTP+SSE dual-endpoint transport.

nodes.label: Host injects tool result for LLM
nodes.rationale: MCP server replies to host MCP client, not directly to the LLM. Host wraps result in tool-calling loop; LLM never opens a socket to the MCP server.
nodes.norm_label: host injects tool result for llm

nodes.label: MCP vs native connectors vs REST
nodes.rationale: MCP value is one connector format many hosts can reuse. MCP does not replace permission models of underlying APIs.
nodes.norm_label: mcp vs native connectors vs rest

nodes.label: MCP security checklist
nodes.rationale: Env vars for API keys; enable only needed servers; HTTPS for remote; limit scopes against prompt injection → tool abuse.

nodes.label: Vector database
nodes.rationale: Stores embeddings for meaning-similarity search over large text; retrieval engine in RAG. Not part of MCP spec — optional backend behind search tool or product RAG.

nodes.label: Skills vs vector DB vs MCP decision
nodes.rationale: Skills = playbook; Vector DB = semantic memory over documents; MCP = live hands into systems. Common production pattern: RAG for knowledge, MCP for actions.
nodes.norm_label: skills vs vector db vs mcp decision

nodes.label: Custom MCP server
nodes.rationale: Build when you have internal API/DB only your team has, or repeatable agent actions; use existing servers or Skills otherwise.

nodes.label: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk (TypeScript)
nodes.rationale: Best when JS/TS monorepo or npm publish; Zod schemas; matches Cursor examples.

nodes.label: FastMCP (Python)
nodes.rationale: Fastest if logic already in Python scripts or data libs; type hints/Pydantic; Python 3.10+.

nodes.label: Plan your server
nodes.rationale: Before coding, decide what the agent may do and must never do; MCP servers are small connectors.

nodes.label: MCP Tool
nodes.rationale: Function the model calls with arguments; start with tools only — cover ~90% of custom integrations.

nodes.label: MCP Resource
nodes.rationale: Readable URI the user or model can fetch; add when agent should read stable documents.

nodes.label: MCP Prompt
nodes.rationale: Pre-built template the host can insert; reusable slash-command style.

nodes.label: stdio transport
nodes.rationale: Default local transport — host spawns process; fastest path for Cursor/Claude Desktop.

nodes.label: Streamable HTTP
nodes.rationale: Team-hosted connector / remote agents transport.

nodes.label: One server, one integration
nodes.rationale: Fewer clearer tools improve LLM tool choice; avoid mega-servers combining CRM+GitHub+email+shell.

nodes.label: Configuration and secrets
nodes.rationale: API keys via env vars from mcp.json; never commit tokens.

nodes.label: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
nodes.rationale: Official TypeScript SDK wraps JSON-RPC and stdio so you register tools instead of parsing raw messages.

nodes.label: FastMCP
nodes.rationale: Python MCP scaffold — fastest for scripts; from mcp.server.fastmcp.

nodes.label: Zod
nodes.rationale: TypeScript input validation for tool schemas.

nodes.label: echo tool
nodes.rationale: Smoke-test tool that echoes text back.

nodes.label: Tool design rules
nodes.rationale: Verb-first names, rich descriptions, small inputs, bounded output, explicit read/write.

nodes.label: Tool result content blocks
nodes.rationale: MCP tools return content blocks — usually text; JSON.stringify for structured data.

nodes.label: isError tool result
nodes.rationale: Return validation/API errors with isError true so the model can retry.

nodes.label: MCP anti-patterns
nodes.rationale: Avoid arbitrary SQL, run_shell with full bash, 10MB JSON returns, case-only name differences.

nodes.label: MCP Inspector
nodes.rationale: Official inspector talks to server over stdio without Cursor — verify tools/schemas first.

nodes.label: Cursor mcp.json
nodes.rationale: Project-level .cursor/mcp.json configures command, args, and env for MCP servers.

nodes.label: claude_desktop_config.json
nodes.rationale: Same server binary works with Claude Desktop via mcpServers config.

nodes.label: stderr logging for stdio servers
nodes.rationale: Never console.log to stdout — stdout is JSON-RPC wire; log to stderr.

nodes.label: Least-privilege tools
nodes.rationale: Separate read vs write; allowlisted actions; rate limiting; scoped API keys.

nodes.label: MCP vs Skills
nodes.rationale: MCP holds live data/APIs/mutations; Skill holds how the team wants the agent to use those tools.

nodes.label: Prompt injection tool abuse
nodes.rationale: Narrow tools; no arbitrary code execution; confirm writes in UI.

nodes.label: npm MCP distribution
nodes.rationale: Publish TS servers as npx -y @org/package for team install.

nodes.label: Implementation examples — overview
nodes.rationale: Hands-on notes for running open models locally — HF download, runtime, RAM, CPU runners.

nodes.label: Local inference pipeline
nodes.rationale: Hugging Face repo → weights on disk → runtime → API or UI.

nodes.label: Qwen2.5-Coder 7B
nodes.rationale: Default coding pick — open license, no HF gating, fits 8 GB GPUs; ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:7b.

nodes.label: Local run vs hosted API
nodes.rationale: Run locally for data residency, cost at volume, offline; use API for frontier models and minimal setup.

nodes.label: Hugging Face
nodes.rationale: Hosts model weights, tokenizers, and configs as versioned repos.

nodes.label: hf CLI
nodes.rationale: Current Hugging Face CLI (huggingface-cli deprecated); hf download, hf auth login.

nodes.label: GGUF
nodes.rationale: Quantized weight format for llama.cpp / Ollama imports.

nodes.label: safetensors
nodes.rationale: Full-precision or HF-quantized weights for Python runtimes (transformers/vLLM).

nodes.label: Gated HF models
nodes.rationale: Require web license approval + hf auth login; e.g. meta-llama/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct.

nodes.label: snapshot_download
nodes.rationale: Python huggingface_hub API to download repos in scripts/notebooks.

nodes.label: Ollama
nodes.rationale: Fast local start; OpenAI-compatible /v1; one-command pull; wraps llama.cpp.

nodes.label: llama.cpp
nodes.rationale: Maximum GGUF control; strong CPU; llama-server HTTP.

nodes.label: vLLM
nodes.rationale: Production GPU serving with PagedAttention; NVIDIA required.

nodes.label: MLX
nodes.rationale: Apple Silicon Metal kernels.

nodes.label: OpenAI-compatible local API
nodes.rationale: Most local stacks expose OpenAI-compatible HTTP so Cursor/Continue work.

nodes.label: KV cache
nodes.rationale: Attention state for prompt + generation; grows with context length and batch size.

nodes.label: Model weights memory
nodes.rationale: GB ≈ params(B) × bytes/param × 1.07; quantization reduces footprint.

nodes.label: Q4_K_M quantization
nodes.rationale: Good default GGUF quant — ~4.5 GB weights for 7B; suited to 8–16 GB machines.

nodes.label: Partial GPU offload
nodes.rationale: llama.cpp -ngl: some layers on GPU, rest in RAM.

nodes.label: airLLM
nodes.rationale: Streams one transformer layer at a time through GPU — 70B-class on 4 GB VRAM but slow.

nodes.label: llamafile
nodes.rationale: Model + runtime in one file; drop-in executable.

nodes.label: Layer streaming
nodes.rationale: Keep full weights in system RAM; move one layer to GPU per forward step.

nodes.label: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 1080
nodes.rationale: 8 GB VRAM Pascal sm_61; comfortable with 3B–7B Q4; vLLM/TGI/TensorRT-LLM poorly supported.

nodes.label: Ollama Modelfile
nodes.rationale: Import custom GGUF via FROM path and ollama create.

nodes.label: Pascal GPU limits for modern runtimes
nodes.rationale: vLLM/TGI/TensorRT-LLM target newer GPUs; prefer Ollama or llama.cpp on RTX 1080.
nodes.norm_label: pascal gpu limits for modern runtimes

nodes.label: TurboVec
nodes.rationale: Compresses vectors (TurboQuant 4-bit) and runs local similarity search; not an MCP server by itself.

nodes.label: nomic-embed-text
nodes.rationale: Ollama embedding model — 768-dim vectors for TurboVec default path.

nodes.label: Fully local RAG
nodes.rationale: Files on disk → chunk → Ollama embed → TurboVec index → Ollama LLM answers.

nodes.label: Loop prompting
nodes.rationale: Work with AI in cycles instead of restarting from zero; invest once in durable instructions.

nodes.label: Human-in-the-loop
nodes.rationale: Keep one session/project; send short deltas like 'Shorter intro.'

nodes.label: Time / event loop
nodes.rationale: Arm recurring or watcher trigger — e.g. Cursor /loop 5m check CI.

nodes.label: One-shot prompting
nodes.rationale: Treats every chat as blank slate; expensive for recurring work.

nodes.label: Delta message
nodes.rationale: Correction, new attachment, or changed input — not a rebuilt system prompt.

nodes.label: Promote repeated paragraphs
nodes.rationale: If you typed the same paragraph twice this week, promote it to persistent instructions.

nodes.label: Persistent instructions
nodes.rationale: The prompt you don't repeat — loaded automatically when the product thinks they apply.

nodes.label: Three times promotion rule
nodes.rationale: If you have sent it three times, externalize it to persistent layer.

nodes.label: Session loop
nodes.rationale: Refine same deliverable in one thread with one change per message.

nodes.label: Cursor /loop
nodes.rationale: Runs a prompt on an interval — arm → wake → act → re-arm.

nodes.label: Event-driven loop
nodes.rationale: Wake on PR opened, build failed, file saved — not blind polling.

nodes.label: Context rot
nodes.rationale: Stored context lies or threads fill with old turns — model forgets rules or contradicts itself.

nodes.label: Reset thread vs instructions
nodes.rationale: New chat + same project often fixes thread rot without losing persistent instructions.

nodes.label: Loop verification habits
nodes.rationale: Loops amplify mistakes — verify sources, diff before accept, human gate, log output.

nodes.label: Multimodal inputs
nodes.rationale: Modern AI tools accept images, PDFs, spreadsheets, audio, screenshots — not just typed prompts.

nodes.label: PDF page scoping
nodes.rationale: Limit questions to page ranges; long PDFs may truncate.

nodes.label: Spreadsheets and data
nodes.rationale: AI may hallucinate rows — spot-check against source; redact PII first.

nodes.label: Hallucinated spreadsheet rows
nodes.rationale: Spot-check AI-produced rows against source before decisions.

nodes.label: Voice and meetings workflow
nodes.rationale: Record → transcribe → summarise; extract tasks with owners; review before sharing.

nodes.label: ollama pull / run / serve
nodes.rationale: pull caches weights; run loads chat CLI; serve HTTP API on :11434 OpenAI-compatible.

nodes.label: Ollama
nodes.rationale: Best first choice for local coding with Cursor, offline chat, quick experiments without HF gating.

nodes.label: CUDA (bundled runtime)
nodes.rationale: Ollama bundles its own CUDA runtime — you do not need a separate CUDA toolkit install for basic use.

nodes.label: Local RAG with Ollama embeddings
nodes.rationale: Use the same Ollama base URL for embed and chat in your app.

nodes.label: Runtime parameters (temperature, num_ctx, top_p)
nodes.rationale: Coding tasks: try temperature 0.1–0.3; raise num_ctx if you have VRAM.

nodes.label: Local API security (no auth)
nodes.rationale: No API auth locally — keep 127.0.0.1 unless intending remote access; do not expose :11434 to the public internet.

nodes.label: Modelfile
nodes.rationale: Use when model is not in the Ollama library or you want custom system prompt and parameters baked in.

nodes.label: CUDA OOM / VRAM pressure
nodes.rationale: Fix with smaller model tag, lower num_ctx, or unload idle models; VRAM guide in Model RAM requirements.

nodes.label: Skills (SKILL.md workflows)
nodes.rationale: Encode team rules once so agents stop needing re-explanation every chat; product loads them when the task matches.

nodes.label: MCP / connectors
nodes.rationale: Skills tell the agent your process; MCP gives live API access — use both.

nodes.label: Project skills (.cursor/skills/)
nodes.rationale: Team process → repo (commit and PR); personal habits → home folder (never commit).

nodes.label: Cross-tool portable setup
nodes.rationale: Same idea works across tools but folder paths differ; AGENTS.md is the cross-tool README for agents; SKILL.md is an open Agent Skills pattern.

nodes.label: Skill sync strategies (duplicate/symlink/docs)
nodes.rationale: Duplicate for small teams; docs/skills/ canonical + script for CI; symlink local-only; paste Cursor rules into AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md for other tools.

nodes.label: Rules vs skills (Cursor)
nodes.rationale: Rules for how code should look (always/glob); skills for how to run a multi-step process (task-matched description).

nodes.label: Linking a fixed script
nodes.rationale: Scripts are real files under scripts/ — not embedded in SKILL.md; agent runs them via Shell when the workflow matches.

nodes.label: Cursor hooks (.cursor/hooks.json)
nodes.rationale: Hook fires on events without user asking; skill+script for procedures; MCP for live data.

nodes.label: Progressive disclosure
nodes.rationale: Keep SKILL.md under ~500 lines; put rare edge cases in reference.md so the agent reads the minimum first.

nodes.label: Skill description triggers
nodes.rationale: Description must say what it does and when, with synonyms users actually say — drives auto-load.

nodes.label: Shared run_log.py JSON logging
nodes.rationale: Prefer Python stdlib json logging over bash for structured runtime logs without jq.

nodes.label: Parameterized script + clarify pattern
nodes.rationale: Ask for missing parameters and confirm intent before executing; especially for production.

nodes.label: Loop on script results pattern
nodes.rationale: Keep current_log_file as source of truth; refine across iterations without re-fetching from scratch.

nodes.label: Agent orchestration
nodes.rationale: Coordinate AGENTS.md, skills, hooks, and scripts so the right workflow runs at the right time.

nodes.label: AGENTS.md (agent briefing)
nodes.rationale: Always-on briefing loaded every agent session; do not merge with skills or hooks into one file.

nodes.label: Skill layer
nodes.rationale: Runs when user prompt matches skill description; started by user or explicit invoke.

nodes.label: Hook layer
nodes.rationale: Runs on product events (shell, commit, edit, stop) with no user prompt.

nodes.label: Three orchestration layers
nodes.rationale: Do not merge AGENTS.md, skills, and hooks into one file; each has its own trigger and job.

nodes.label: Skill (on-demand workflow)
nodes.rationale: Nothing happens until user task matches description; advisory unless also gated by a hook.

nodes.label: Skill + script pattern
nodes.rationale: Skill says WHEN + HOW to ask user; script does deterministic work + JSON log; agent reads log and summarizes.

nodes.label: AGENTS.md standing briefing
nodes.rationale: Loads automatically at session start; keep short; put facts here and procedures in skills.

nodes.label: Facts vs procedures split
nodes.rationale: Put facts in AGENTS.md; put procedures in skills.

nodes.label: Hooks (event gates)
nodes.rationale: Automatic local/dev-time gates; not a replacement for CI; agent not in loop unless blocked.

nodes.label: failClosed
nodes.rationale: Block commit if hook crashes or times out.

nodes.label: Hook + companion skill pattern
nodes.rationale: Hooks stay small and deterministic; put explanation and remediation in a skill.

nodes.label: Layered responsibility matrix
nodes.rationale: Use each layer for one job: AGENTS.md for facts, skills for procedures, hooks for enforcement.

nodes.label: Combine-layer anti-patterns
nodes.rationale: Do not put full PR checklist in AGENTS.md; do not put commit rules only in a skill; do not make hook call the LLM.

nodes.label: Orchestration stack
nodes.rationale: Human → AGENTS.md → Hooks → Skills → Scripts + MCP; each layer orchestrates a different concern.

nodes.label: Gate then explain pattern
nodes.rationale: Hook enforces; companion skill narrates; user fixes and retries.

nodes.label: Script loop pattern
nodes.rationale: Skill runs script → log; agent proposes fix; user approves; skill re-runs and compares logs.

nodes.label: MCP + skills pattern
nodes.rationale: Skill without MCP is a stale runbook; MCP without skill means agent improvises process.

nodes.label: Hooks stay dumb design rule
nodes.rationale: Fast and auditable; no model latency on every commit.

nodes.label: hook-failure-help skill
nodes.rationale: Hooks do not explain themselves in prose — this skill does; do not suggest bypassing failClosed.

nodes.label: pr-review-lite SKILL.md
nodes.rationale: Does not run on commit; use a hook for gates, not this skill.

nodes.label: Tools & orchestration
nodes.rationale: Connecting AI to where you already work so tasks flow without copy-pasting between tabs.

nodes.label: AI tool map
nodes.rationale: Pick one primary chat and one primary coding assistant to avoid context sprawl.

nodes.label: Copy-paste bridge
nodes.rationale: Lowest friction; fine for occasional tasks; does not scale.

nodes.label: IDE + repo context
nodes.rationale: Keep README/rules accurate; small scoped tasks; use @file mentions.

nodes.label: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
nodes.rationale: JSON-RPC over stdio or HTTP — not gRPC; server calls product REST/HTTPS APIs.

nodes.label: Automation chains
nodes.rationale: Put human approval steps before external sends.

nodes.label: Model switching
nodes.rationale: Draft cheap with mini model → polish with flagship on final pass.

nodes.label: Tools & orchestration anti-patterns
nodes.rationale: Avoid spreading sensitive docs across free-tier chats; avoid unreviewed agent repo write; avoid unreviewed automation posts.

nodes.label: Trust, privacy & verify
nodes.rationale: AI tools are fast and fluent — not automatically true, private, or allowed for the workplace.

nodes.label: Hallucination
nodes.rationale: Plausible-sounding output that is wrong — fake citations, wrong numbers, invented API names.

nodes.label: What not to paste
nodes.rationale: Never paste passwords, unredacted PII, MNPI, patient/student records, or privileged docs unless enterprise-approved.

nodes.label: Enterprise vs consumer tiers
nodes.rationale: Use company-approved tools with DPA and no training on data for work data.

nodes.label: Prompt injection (user angle)
nodes.rationale: Malicious pasted content may say ignore previous instructions; delimit untrusted text and treat as data.

nodes.label: Agent automation risks
nodes.rationale: Agents can act: require approval before send, narrow permissions, draft-only roles, disable purchases or require 2FA.

nodes.label: Bias and fairness
nodes.rationale: For HR, lending, medical, or legal-adjacent use: human review; do not fully automate high-stakes decisions.

nodes.label: Deep learning
nodes.rationale: Subset of ML using neural networks with many layers; prefer for images, long text, audio with large data and compute.

nodes.label: Classical ML vs deep learning
nodes.rationale: Prefer classical ML for small tabular data and interpretable coefficients; many systems use both.

nodes.label: Activation functions
nodes.rationale: Non-linearity required; without it a stack of layers collapses to linear.

nodes.label: Mini-batch training
nodes.rationale: GPU-efficient; noisy gradient helps generalisation; epoch is one full pass.

nodes.label: CNN (Convolutional Neural Network)
nodes.rationale: Local connectivity, weight sharing, and hierarchy suit spatial/image data.

nodes.label: LSTM
nodes.rationale: Gates fix vanishing gradient on long sequences.

nodes.label: ResNet
nodes.rationale: Skip connections fix vanishing gradient in deep CNNs.

nodes.label: Transformer (vs CNN/RNN)
nodes.rationale: Global attention dominates NLP and much of vision; RNNs mostly replaced in NLP.

nodes.label: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017)
nodes.norm_label: attention is all you need (vaswani et al., 2017)

nodes.label: Self-attention
nodes.rationale: Each token builds a representation by attending to all other tokens; output is a weighted sum of values with weights from query-key similarity.

nodes.label: Multi-head attention
nodes.rationale: Run h attention heads in parallel with different learned projections to capture different relationship types (syntax, coreference).

nodes.label: Positional encoding
nodes.rationale: Attention alone is permutation-invariant — order must be injected via sinusoidal encodings or learned embeddings.

nodes.label: Decoder-only architecture
nodes.rationale: Causal mask — token t sees only ≤ t; LLMs for chat are almost always decoder-only causal LMs (GPT, LLaMA).

nodes.label: Large language models (LLMs)
nodes.rationale: Very large decoder-only transformers trained on massive text to predict the next token; alignment and prompting make them useful assistants.

nodes.label: LLM lifecycle
nodes.rationale: Pre-train (next token) → SFT (instructions) → RLHF/DPO (preferences) → Deploy (+ RAG / LoRA).

nodes.label: Causal language modelling
nodes.rationale: Given tokens [t1…tn], predict tn+1 left-to-right; loss is cross-entropy over vocabulary at each position.

nodes.label: Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE)
nodes.rationale: Merge frequent byte/character pairs into a subword vocabulary (30k–100k tokens); affects billing, context limits, and robustness.

nodes.label: Alignment
nodes.rationale: Pre-trained models predict likely text — not necessarily helpful or safe; alignment trains behaviour users expect from assistants.

nodes.label: RLHF
nodes.rationale: Humans rank outputs → train reward model → fine-tune LLM with PPO; aligns preferences but expensive and risk of reward hacking.

nodes.label: Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO)
nodes.rationale: Train directly on (prompt, chosen, rejected) pairs with no separate reward model — simpler pipeline than RLHF.

nodes.label: Prompt engineering
nodes.rationale: Model weights are fixed at inference; prompting steers behaviour through input text — system instructions, examples, and output format.

nodes.label: Prompt injection
nodes.rationale: Attack where untrusted text in the prompt overrides system instructions; mitigate by separating untrusted blocks, filtering, least privilege.

nodes.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
nodes.rationale: Embed docs → vector store → retrieve top-k chunks into prompt; update knowledge by changing index without full retrain; quality equals retrieval quality.

nodes.label: LoRA
nodes.rationale: Low-rank adapters on attention layers — train <1% params; production often combines LoRA for format with RAG for facts.

nodes.label: Guardrails stack
nodes.rationale: Layer defences: input filter → LLM → output filter; no single filter catches everything.

nodes.label: Machine learning (ML)
nodes.rationale: Builds systems that improve from data instead of only hand-written rules; parameters adjust so predictions improve on unseen inputs.

nodes.label: Train / validation / test split
nodes.rationale: Never tune on the test set — it becomes validation by accident; test used once for final unbiased estimate.

nodes.label: Bias-variance tradeoff
nodes.rationale: Underfitting = high bias (both errors high); overfitting = high variance (train low, test high); regularization and tuning find the sweet spot.

nodes.label: Feature leakage
nodes.rationale: Feature contains information unavailable at prediction time or encodes the label; symptom is too-good offline metrics and production collapse.

nodes.label: Cryptocurrency
nodes.rationale: Digital value on a shared ledger where ownership is enforced by cryptography, not a single company database.

nodes.label: Smart contract
nodes.rationale: Program code deployed on the blockchain that runs when users send transactions; lives on-chain — not hosted on a VPS.

nodes.label: UTXO model
nodes.rationale: Ledger is a set of unspent outputs; spend consumes whole outputs and creates new ones (change); used by Bitcoin and Cardano eUTXO.

nodes.label: Account model
nodes.rationale: Global per-address balance and contract storage; used by Ethereum, BNB Chain, Tron, TON.

nodes.label: Fee split pattern
nodes.rationale: fee = amount × feeBps / 10000 → feeAccount; remainder = amount − fee → recipient; shared business rule across BNB, Tron, TON, Cardano.

nodes.label: On-chain deploy (no VPS hosting)
nodes.rationale: Smart contracts live on the blockchain replicated on nodes; pay once to deploy then gas per call; server optional only for website/UI.

nodes.label: Failed transactions still cost fees
nodes.rationale: Reverted on-chain executions usually still charge gas for work done; rejected before broadcast typically costs nothing.

nodes.label: Two buckets of funds (payment + gas)
nodes.rationale: Users need payment amount plus network fee; confusing them is the most common insufficient-funds mistake on EVM chains.

nodes.label: Safe vs completed verification
nodes.rationale: Safe = signing the right thing before broadcast; completed = included in a block and succeeded (not reverted/bounced).

nodes.label: Verify before broadcast
nodes.rationale: Catch logic/config errors before mainnet fees: compile → test → simulate → testnet → mainnet; cannot guarantee zero failures (congestion, MEV).

nodes.label: Pre-flight checklist
nodes.rationale: Nine gates from compile through mainnet canary so most failures happen off-chain or on cheap testnets.

nodes.label: 2% fee split pattern
nodes.rationale: Incoming payment: fee = amount × 200/10000 to app account; remainder to recipient.

nodes.label: immutable appAccount
nodes.rationale: Assigned once in constructor and baked into bytecode; no setter after deploy.

nodes.label: SendPayGasSeparately
nodes.rationale: Gas paid separately so contract keeps little TON; gas comes from attached value.

nodes.label: eUTXO model
nodes.rationale: Value lives in outputs; validators check txs; split via fee and recipient outputs, not contract balance.

nodes.label: Account + message model
nodes.rationale: Contracts communicate via messages; value in context().value; outputs via send().

nodes.label: Energy and Bandwidth
nodes.rationale: Tron uses Energy/Bandwidth instead of ETH-style gas; stake TRX or burn for execution.

nodes.label: Temporal and spatial locality
nodes.rationale: Good locality keeps hot data in registers and cache; hierarchy trades capacity vs latency.

nodes.label: Big O notation & time complexity in JavaScript
nodes.norm_label: big o notation & time complexity in javascript

nodes.label: DP vs greedy decision checklist
nodes.rationale: Greedy if local choices proven safe; DP when overlapping structured subproblems.

nodes.label: Sort stability
nodes.rationale: Stable sorts keep equal keys' relative order; Arrays.sort(Object[]) uses TimSort for stability.

nodes.label: Binary search on answer
nodes.rationale: Search minimum x where monotone predicate P(x) flips; not array indices.

nodes.label: Choose–recurse–unchoose
nodes.rationale: Systematic trial and error on implicit DFS state tree; undo on dead ends.

nodes.label: Greedy choice property
nodes.rationale: Prove some optimal solution uses greedy first pick, then optimal substructure; else use DP.

nodes.label: Optimal substructure
nodes.rationale: Requires optimal substructure plus overlapping subproblems; reuse via memo or table.

nodes.label: Solving with the JDK
nodes.rationale: In coursework you implement algorithms by hand; in production Java you compose java.util and Arrays ADTs instead of reimplementing them.

nodes.label: Array (static)
nodes.rationale: Contiguous indexed storage; O(1) random access via base+i×wordSize; fixed length once allocated in Java.

nodes.label: Dynamic array (ArrayList)
nodes.rationale: Growable contiguous buffer; resize copies when full (~50% growth); amortized O(1) append.

nodes.label: Linked list
nodes.rationale: Pointer-based sequence; O(1) splice at known node; arrays win on O(1) index and cache behavior.

nodes.label: Stack ADT
nodes.rationale: LIFO defined by operations (push/pop/peek), not by backing structure.

nodes.label: Deque + ArrayDeque as stack
nodes.rationale: Prefer Deque/ArrayDeque over legacy java.util.Stack (synchronized Vector subclass).

nodes.label: Queue ADT
nodes.rationale: FIFO linear ADT: enqueue at back, dequeue from front.

nodes.label: Circular buffer (ring queue)
nodes.rationale: Wrap indices with modulo capacity to avoid O(n) shifting on dequeue.

nodes.label: Deque (double-ended queue)
nodes.rationale: Insert/remove at both ends in O(1); generalizes stack and queue.

nodes.label: Binary search tree (BST)
nodes.rationale: Left keys < node < right keys; ops O(h); skewed inserts give Θ(n) height.

nodes.label: Binary heap
nodes.rationale: Complete binary tree in array; insert/extract O(log n); buildHeap O(n).

nodes.label: Priority queue
nodes.rationale: Extract by priority (min/max key), not arrival time (FIFO) or recency (LIFO).

nodes.label: decrease-key
nodes.rationale: Standard PriorityQueue lacks efficient decrease-key; need indexed heap or Fibonacci heap for fast Dijkstra variants.

nodes.label: Hash table
nodes.rationale: Hash function maps keys to slots; average O(1) with good hash and load control.

nodes.label: Database / DBMS
nodes.rationale: Organized storage with CRUD, concurrency, durability, and query support beyond flat files.

nodes.label: Embedding vs referencing
nodes.rationale: Embed for one-to-few co-read data; reference for huge one-to-many or shared sub-documents.

nodes.label: Wide-column database
nodes.rationale: Partition by key for massive write throughput; query-first modeling and denormalization are normal.

nodes.label: Index-free adjacency
nodes.rationale: Each node points to its edges so local multi-hop traversal is cheap vs deep SQL JOIN chains.

nodes.label: Tag cardinality
nodes.rationale: High-cardinality tags (user_id, request_id) explode storage; prefer bounded label sets.

nodes.label: UDP
nodes.rationale: Connectionless best-effort datagrams; low latency when loss is acceptable.

nodes.label: TCP
nodes.rationale: Connection-oriented reliable ordered byte stream with three-way handshake.

nodes.label: TLS
nodes.rationale: Confidentiality, integrity, and server authentication via public-key crypto and X.509 certificates.

nodes.label: Forward secrecy
nodes.rationale: Ephemeral Diffie–Hellman/ECDH so long-term key compromise does not decrypt old sessions.

nodes.label: TTL and caching
nodes.rationale: Low TTL speeds failover and migrations; high TTL reduces load but slows cutover.

nodes.label: Kubernetes Ingress
nodes.rationale: Ingress is not DNS — DNS picks IP; Ingress routes by Host and path after TCP arrives.

nodes.label: TLS at the edge
nodes.rationale: Terminate at ingress for simplicity (may need mTLS inside); pass-through when SNI routing is at a smarter LB.

nodes.label: Geo DNS
nodes.rationale: Geo DNS alone only picks the front door; compute and data must move with the user for residency and latency.

nodes.label: Regional subdomains
nodes.rationale: Prefer for data residency and partner docs; clearer than geo guesswork.

nodes.label: TLS termination modes
nodes.rationale: Terminate at edge, passthrough, or re-encrypt (SSL bridging) change what L4 vs L7 can see.

nodes.label: Session affinity (stickiness)
nodes.rationale: Later frames on the same TCP connection must reach the same backend that accepted the Upgrade; or use pub/sub backplane.

nodes.label: Defense in depth
nodes.rationale: Layer controls so one failure does not mean total compromise; aim for proportionate controls.

nodes.label: Risk = likelihood × impact
nodes.rationale: Likely + high impact is stop-ship; unlikely + high impact still may need early mitigation.

nodes.label: Zero trust
nodes.rationale: Never trust, always verify — being on VPN is not enough; identity per request + least network access.

nodes.label: Tabletop exercise
nodes.rationale: Tests human/process aspects of IR — roles, comms, runbooks — not just monitoring detection.

nodes.label: Immutable backups
nodes.rationale: Ransomware recovery path — attacker cannot encrypt backups too.

nodes.label: Hardware bypass
nodes.rationale: Abuse how physical systems work (radios, phones, key fobs) instead of breaking software crypto.

nodes.label: Rolling codes
nodes.rationale: Stop replay of captured unlock frames; do not stop live relay because the real key signs a fresh challenge.

nodes.label: OTP bombing
nodes.rationale: Abuses your own security stack — each OTP SMS is legitimate from the app's view.

nodes.label: Downgrade attack
nodes.rationale: Stack designed to keep working when conditions are poor — force strong mode to weak legacy mode.

nodes.label: Distance bounding
nodes.rationale: Cryptographically estimates time of flight — relay adds too much latency to fake near.

nodes.label: Earn links, don't buy them
nodes.rationale: Paid link schemes violate search guidelines and risk penalties.

nodes.label: Minimum viable measurement
nodes.rationale: Search Console + GA4 + 1–3 conversion events + UTM discipline before scaling spend.

nodes.label: Attribution models
nodes.rationale: No model is true — last-click for ops simplicity; data-driven when budget is large; last-click undervalues content.

nodes.label: A/B testing
nodes.rationale: One change at a time; enough traffic; full business weeks; predefine success metric.

nodes.label: Topical authority
nodes.rationale: Sites that cover a topic completely and consistently often rank new URLs in that topic faster.

nodes.label: Editorial calendar
nodes.rationale: Turns strategy into shipped pages with briefs, owners, and review so quality stays consistent.

nodes.label: Content brief
nodes.rationale: Briefs prevent starting writing without intent, ICP, outline, links, CTA, and success metrics.

nodes.label: Content governance
nodes.rationale: Style guide, YMYL fact-check, version history, and retirement/301 policy protect brand and trust.

nodes.label: Content distribution
nodes.rationale: Publishing without distribution hides content; distribute across owned channels without harming SEO.

nodes.label: Canonical URL
nodes.rationale: One canonical URL on your site; derivatives drive traffic back; full third-party duplicates compete with your URL.

nodes.label: Syndication rules
nodes.rationale: Syndicate with rel=canonical or publish excerpt + link; avoid full duplicate off-site.

nodes.label: Email marketing
nodes.rationale: Owned channel: reach opted-in people without paying per send or fighting an algorithm.

nodes.label: List building
nodes.rationale: Permission-based lists outperform purchased lists; bought lists violate ESP ToS and destroy deliverability.

nodes.label: Double opt-in
nodes.rationale: Cleaner list and proof of consent (GDPR-friendly) at the cost of friction.

nodes.label: List hygiene
nodes.rationale: Remove hard bounces and sunset inactive subscribers so ISPs do not penalize senders.

nodes.label: Email segmentation
nodes.rationale: Even two segments beat blast-to-all; balance roughly 80/20 value to promotion.

nodes.label: One primary CTA
nodes.rationale: One goal per email; competing CTAs dilute action.

nodes.label: Dedicated sending domain
nodes.rationale: Marketing subdomain (e.g. mail.yourdomain.com) protects main domain if sender reputation issues occur.

nodes.label: Growth loop
nodes.rationale: Understand audience → offer → publish → distribute → measure → iterate.

nodes.label: Owned vs earned vs paid media
nodes.rationale: Compounding channels (SEO, content, email) build assets; paid buys reach on demand after you know what converts.
nodes.norm_label: owned vs earned vs paid media

nodes.label: Primary channel selection
nodes.rationale: Tiny teams cannot excel at every channel; pick one primary and one secondary matched to how buyers discover.

nodes.label: Ideal customer profile (ICP)
nodes.rationale: Narrow beats broad; marketing fails when it speaks to everyone.

nodes.label: Jobs to be done (JTBD)
nodes.rationale: People hire products to make progress; lead with outcome not feature lists.

nodes.label: North-star metric
nodes.rationale: Single outcome reflecting delivered customer value; marketing supports it and is rarely identical to traffic.

nodes.label: Vanity vs useful metrics
nodes.rationale: Prefer traffic to money pages, link clicks, click rate + revenue per send over raw traffic, followers, or list size alone.

nodes.label: White-hat link building
nodes.rationale: Aligns with guidelines; black-hat short-term wins often end in lost traffic.

nodes.label: Link-building loop
nodes.rationale: Asset → prospect → outreach → earn → measure; skip asset and outreach feels like spam.

nodes.label: Exact-match anchor overuse
nodes.rationale: Overuse looks manipulative; prefer branded or descriptive anchors.

nodes.label: Disavow file
nodes.rationale: Use sparingly only with evidence of harm or manual action; incorrect disavow can hurt legitimate links.

nodes.label: Search intent
nodes.rationale: Modern SEO targets what the user wants, not keyword density; one primary intent per URL.

nodes.label: Meta description
nodes.rationale: Not a direct ranking factor for most queries but affects CTR.

nodes.label: Paid advertising
nodes.rationale: Buys reach on demand; accelerates what works; rarely fixes a broken offer or untracked funnel.

nodes.label: Paid vs organic mindset
nodes.rationale: Organic/SEO is an asset that ranks for months; paid is a throttle that stops when budget stops.

nodes.label: Account structure (campaign → ad group → ads/keywords)
nodes.rationale: Tight keyword themes per ad group and one primary landing URL per intent keep CPC manageable under competition.

nodes.label: Quality Score
nodes.rationale: Higher Quality Score lowers CPC for the same position via expected CTR, ad relevance, and landing page experience.

nodes.label: Negative keywords
nodes.rationale: Weekly search terms review adds negatives to prevent budget bleed from irrelevant queries.

nodes.label: Landing page alignment
nodes.rationale: Ad promise must match landing page content; mismatch kills conversion rate and Quality Score.

nodes.label: Retargeting
nodes.rationale: Start retargeting before broad cold spend when traffic already exists; warm audiences convert better.

nodes.label: Paid unit economics (CPC, CPA, ROAS, CTR, CVR)
nodes.norm_label: paid unit economics (cpc, cpa, roas, ctr, cvr)

nodes.label: Kill rules
nodes.rationale: Write pause rules for CPA/spend/CTR thresholds before scaling; automate pauses in-platform.

nodes.label: Learning phase and sample size
nodes.rationale: Wait for ~30–50 conversions per major test before big structural changes; daily tinkering resets learning.

nodes.label: Budget allocation (test → validate → scale)
nodes.rationale: Increase 15–30% every few days when scaling; keep 10–20% budget for experiments.

nodes.label: Conversion API (server-side)
nodes.rationale: Server-side events reduce gaps from ad blockers, iOS ATT, and consent decline but do not fix everything.

nodes.label: MER (marketing efficiency ratio)
nodes.rationale: Total revenue ÷ total marketing spend as a blended sanity check when platform ROAS exceeds GA4.

nodes.label: First-party data strategy
nodes.rationale: Email lists, site behavior, and purchase history become primary as third-party cookies decline.

nodes.label: Gram scale tare workflow
nodes.rationale: All weights in grams; tare between ingredients for accuracy without measuring cups.

nodes.label: Cross-links between notes
nodes.rationale: Use relative markdown links with target subtitle as link text; avoid bare backtick filenames.

nodes.label: Local browse vs GitHub vault login
nodes.rationale: Local folder for offline editing; GitHub login for integrated vault creation and remote sync.
nodes.norm_label: local browse vs github vault login

nodes.label: GitHub OAuth scopes / PAT
nodes.rationale: Private repos need repo Contents access; prefer fine-grained PAT Contents:Read-only over broad classic repo scope when possible.

nodes.label: First common kanji (calendar, dates, weekdays, documents)
nodes.norm_label: first common kanji (calendar, dates, weekdays, documents)

nodes.label: Frequent IT kanji
nodes.rationale: Focus on high-frequency building-block kanji in compounds; learn meaning first, on-yomi dominates IT text.

nodes.label: 基本設計書 / 詳細設計書 kanji
nodes.norm_label: 基本設計書 / 詳細設計書 kanji

nodes.label: Office replies (positive / negative)
nodes.rationale: Japanese offices soften no; acknowledge first then act; pair はい with what you will do.

nodes.label: Core particles (を, が, に, の)
nodes.norm_label: core particles (を, か, に, の)

nodes.label: Line hierarchy (部・課・係)
nodes.rationale: Japanese titles do not map 1:1 to Western VP/Director/Manager grades.

nodes.label: 尊敬語 (sonkeigo)
nodes.rationale: Elevate the other person's actions (client/boss); use for their verbs.

nodes.label: 謙譲語 (kenjōgo)
nodes.rationale: Humble your own actions toward clients/seniors; いたします is the workhorse form.

nodes.label: お疲れ様です
nodes.rationale: Default office greeting; never use ご苦労様 upward to 部長.

nodes.label: 障害 (production incident)
nodes.rationale: Spoken updates often precede email; apologize first (申し訳ございません), then facts, then action.

nodes.label: Keigo checklist for outages
nodes.rationale: Client outage needs 申し訳ございません + ご迷惑をおかけ; uncertain ETA uses 恐れ入りますが; to 部長 use ご報告 / 承知いたしました.

nodes.label: 承認 (approval / sign-off)
nodes.rationale: Use いただけますか toward clients; お願いします internal.

nodes.label: Continuous Integration (CI)
nodes.rationale: Automatically build and test every commit; integration pain scales with time between merges.

nodes.label: Continuous Delivery
nodes.rationale: Keep the build always releasable to staging.

nodes.label: Continuous Deployment
nodes.rationale: Auto-deploy every green build to production.

nodes.label: Artifacts & caching
nodes.rationale: Never cache build outputs — use artifacts for that; cache keyed by lock-file hash.

nodes.label: OIDC for CI credentials
nodes.rationale: Prefer OIDC short-lived cloud credentials over long-lived API keys — no secret to leak.

nodes.label: Jenkins owns CI · Ansible owns server state
nodes.rationale: Splitting responsibilities keeps deploy logic reusable outside Jenkins; same playbook from Jenkins or laptop for hotfixes.
nodes.norm_label: jenkins owns ci · ansible owns server state

nodes.label: Agentless model
nodes.rationale: No daemon on targets — only SSH + Python.

nodes.label: Idempotency
nodes.rationale: Second run makes no spurious changes; re-running deploy after partial failure is safe.

nodes.label: Check mode (dry run)
nodes.rationale: Shows what would change without applying — useful in PR review stages.

nodes.label: Handlers (notify)
nodes.rationale: Handlers run once at end of play only if a task notifies them and reported changed.

nodes.label: Ansible tags
nodes.rationale: Jenkins uses --tags deploy for fast redeploys.

nodes.label: Variable precedence
nodes.rationale: Defaults in role; environment overrides in group_vars/staging vs group_vars/production; --extra-vars highest.

nodes.label: Ansible Vault
nodes.rationale: Encrypt secrets at rest in Git; never commit vault password — store in Jenkins Credentials.

nodes.label: Dynamic inventory (AWS/Azure/GCP)
nodes.rationale: Pulls live host lists; auto-discovery via tags; terminated instances drop from inventory.

nodes.label: Deploy health checks (wait_for / uri)
nodes.rationale: Fail the playbook if health never passes — Jenkins stage fails, no silent bad deploy.

nodes.label: Artifact handoff (workspace vs Nexus/S3)
nodes.rationale: Option B (registry fetch) scales when deploy agents differ from build agents.

nodes.label: Hotfix without Jenkins (break-glass)
nodes.rationale: Same playbook from laptop; document in runbook — ops shouldn't depend on Jenkins UI during incident.

nodes.label: Pipeline as attack surface
nodes.rationale: Secure CI/CD protects source, build, artifacts, and deploy targets — treat pipeline as part of attack surface.

nodes.label: SLSA levels
nodes.rationale: Most teams target L2 with signed provenance and pinned dependencies; L3 for security-sensitive releases.

nodes.label: Pin actions to commit SHA
nodes.rationale: Floating tags can move; immutable commit SHA prevents compromised action updates.

nodes.label: OIDC federation for cloud credentials
nodes.rationale: Minutes TTL, no long-lived secret stored, scoped per workflow/repo/branch, auditable.

nodes.label: Fork PR secret safety
nodes.rationale: Use pull_request not pull_request_target for untrusted code; GitHub fork PRs get no repo secrets by default.

nodes.label: Least privilege for runners
nodes.rationale: Default to minimum permissions per job — not org-wide admin tokens; separate runner pools by trust zone.

nodes.label: Test pyramid
nodes.rationale: Shift left: fastest cheapest tests on every commit; reserve slow expensive tests for merge or nightly.

nodes.label: Flaky tests
nodes.rationale: Do not disable flaky tests silently — quarantine, track, fix root cause; retries are a band-aid.

nodes.label: Immutable deploy artifacts
nodes.rationale: Deploy by digest or semver tag — never latest in prod.

nodes.label: Feature flags vs hot deploy
nodes.rationale: Flags decouple deploy (safe, frequent) from release (business decision).

nodes.label: Terraform (IaC)
nodes.rationale: Declarative, idempotent, plan-first, provider-based; cloud-agnostic — not an AWS product.

nodes.label: Terraform vs Ansible roles
nodes.rationale: Terraform provisions infra (VPC, DB, K8s); Ansible configures servers (packages, files, services). Common pattern: Terraform creates EC2; Ansible installs app.

nodes.label: Terraform state (tfstate)
nodes.rationale: Maps HCL to real cloud resource IDs; may contain secrets — never commit to Git.

nodes.label: S3 remote backend + DynamoDB locking
nodes.rationale: Teams must use remote backend with locking; without locking concurrent applies corrupt resources.

nodes.label: Terraform modules
nodes.rationale: Package reusable infrastructure; expose inputs and outputs while hiding internal resource names.

nodes.label: Directory-per-environment pattern
nodes.rationale: Recommended for clear separation with different backends, vars, and approval paths.

nodes.label: Terraform workspaces
nodes.rationale: Same code, different state namespaces; works for small teams but can get confusing at scale.

nodes.label: PR plan merge apply pattern
nodes.rationale: Never apply on untrusted PR branches; apply only on main after merge; use OIDC for cloud credentials.

nodes.label: OIDC cloud credentials
nodes.rationale: Avoid static AWS keys in CI by assuming IAM roles via OIDC.

nodes.label: CI/CD platforms
nodes.rationale: Pick based on where code lives, hosting model, and team skills.

nodes.label: GitLab needs DAG
nodes.rationale: Skip strict stage ordering so jobs start when dependencies finish, not when an entire previous stage completes.

nodes.label: Jenkins
nodes.rationale: Choose for on-prem, air-gap, plugin ecosystem, or existing Jenkins investment.

nodes.label: Declarative pipeline
nodes.rationale: Preferred for new projects over Scripted or Freestyle.

nodes.label: Docker in CI
nodes.rationale: Standard pattern: build image, push to registry, deploy by tag.

nodes.label: Multi-stage Dockerfile
nodes.rationale: Builder stage with JDK/Maven is discarded; runtime image keeps only JRE and JAR for a small prod image.

nodes.label: Immutable image tags
nodes.rationale: Prefer GIT_SHA or digest over latest alone in production.

nodes.label: Kaniko
nodes.rationale: Prefer over privileged DinD where policy requires rootless builds.

nodes.label: CircleCI
nodes.rationale: Strong caching and orbs; good for polyrepos wanting SaaS without self-hosting Jenkins.

nodes.label: Tekton
nodes.rationale: Kubernetes-native CI/CD as CRDs; GitOps-friendly; no built-in UI.

nodes.label: CI/CD platform selection
nodes.rationale: No single best tool — match hosting, compliance, team skills, and budget.

nodes.label: Shared responsibility model
nodes.rationale: Security and compliance are always shared — even in SaaS you configure access and protect credentials.

nodes.label: Availability Zone
nodes.rationale: Deploy across at least 2 AZs to survive single datacenter failure.

nodes.label: Multi-region deployment
nodes.rationale: Protects against entire region outage; higher complexity and cost than multi-AZ.

nodes.label: Cold start
nodes.rationale: First invocation after idle incurs latency from sandbox allocation and runtime init; mitigate with provisioned concurrency or SnapStart.

nodes.label: High availability
nodes.rationale: HA minimizes downtime within a region; DR prepares for region-wide disasters.

nodes.label: Stateless services
nodes.rationale: Cloud auto scaling assumes any instance can handle any request; externalize session and uploads.

nodes.label: Microservices
nodes.rationale: Extract only when independent release cadence, scaling profile, or team ownership justifies complexity.

nodes.label: Modular monolith
nodes.rationale: Middle path: clear module boundaries inside one deployable before extracting services.

nodes.label: Event-driven architecture
nodes.rationale: Decouples producers from consumers and absorbs traffic spikes via message brokers.

nodes.label: Saga pattern
nodes.rationale: Distributed transaction across services without a single DB transaction; choreography or orchestration.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.rationale: Single public entry point for north-south client traffic; handles routing, TLS termination, auth, rate limiting, request transformation, and WAF—keep thin, no business logic.

nodes.label: Service mesh
nodes.rationale: East-west sidecar proxies (Envoy) provide uniform retries, mTLS, traffic split, and metrics; adopt when many microservices need uniform policy—not for tiny systems.

nodes.label: Circuit breaker
nodes.rationale: Closed/open/half-open states fail fast on unhealthy downstreams to avoid timeout cascades, retry storms, and cascading waits across services.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.rationale: Combines logs, metrics, and traces so you can operate systems you can see; SLOs turn measurements into actionable targets.

nodes.label: Error budget
nodes.rationale: 100% minus SLO equals allowed bad minutes; when exhausted, freeze features and focus on reliability.

nodes.label: Correlation ID
nodes.rationale: Inject at API Gateway and propagate every hop so one ID reconstructs the full request story across services.

nodes.label: OpenTelemetry
nodes.rationale: Prefer portable instrumentation across clouds; one standard for metrics, logs, and traces with shared traceId correlation.

nodes.label: FinOps
nodes.rationale: Aligns engineering and finance via tagging, budgets, right-sizing, idle cleanup, storage tiering, and showback/chargeback.

nodes.label: Spot / Preemptible instances
nodes.rationale: Cheap interruptible capacity for fault-tolerant batch/ML/CI; pair Spot fleet with on-demand baseline and checkpoint/retry.

nodes.label: SCP / Azure Policy
nodes.rationale: Organization-level deny guardrails that apply even to account admins (e.g. restrict regions, deny root login).

nodes.label: IAM least privilege
nodes.rationale: Prefer scoped actions and resource ARNs, IAM roles/OIDC over long-lived keys, SSO over shared root credentials.

nodes.label: Grafana Loki
nodes.rationale: Indexes labels heavily and compresses log chunks—cheaper than full-text indexing every field.

nodes.label: Alertmanager
nodes.rationale: Open-source routing in the Prometheus ecosystem: dedupe, group, inhibit, silence, and route alerts—does not replace on-call products.

nodes.label: PagerDuty
nodes.rationale: On-call destination configured under receivers; handles schedules and escalations—consumes notifications from Alertmanager.

nodes.label: AlertmanagerConfig CRD
nodes.rationale: Prefer per-namespace RBAC on shared clusters so teams own receivers without one giant global file.

nodes.label: Grafana
nodes.rationale: Pivot UI for incidents: datasources, dashboards, panels; can evaluate alert rules—pick one primary alerting model to avoid double-notify.

nodes.label: Dashboard as code
nodes.rationale: Export or provision dashboard JSON from Git instead of only clicking in prod to avoid drift.

nodes.label: Golden signals
nodes.rationale: Latency, traffic, errors, saturation (or RED/USE) with same layout across services speeds on-call muscle memory.

nodes.label: NetworkPolicy
nodes.rationale: Clusters default allow-all Pod-to-Pod; deny-by-default then allow per app for safer multitenancy.

nodes.label: GitOps
nodes.rationale: Manifests in Git (Flux, Argo CD) with peer review; avoid silent kubectl apply hotfixes that create undebuggable drift.

nodes.label: Multi-stage Docker builds
nodes.rationale: Compile in builder stage; copy only artifacts to runtime for smaller attack surface and faster Node pulls.

nodes.label: Non-root containers
nodes.rationale: Run as USER nobody / runAsNonRoot; prefer readonlyRootFilesystem; never bake secrets into images.

nodes.label: Prometheus
nodes.rationale: Pull-based scraper into local TSDB with PromQL; evaluates alerting rules and forwards to Alertmanager—not a log/trace store.

nodes.label: Prometheus pull model
nodes.rationale: Prometheus scrapes apps on an interval; avoids push back-pressure but requires reachable targets.

nodes.label: Pushgateway
nodes.rationale: For batch/short-lived jobs only—not a generic buffer for long-lived services.

nodes.label: Histogram metric type
nodes.rationale: Prefer histograms over summaries when aggregating latency percentiles across pods via histogram_quantile.

nodes.label: Metric label cardinality
nodes.rationale: Unique label sets create time series; unbounded labels like user_id cause OOM and slow queries.

nodes.label: ServiceMonitor
nodes.rationale: Prefer Operator ServiceMonitor/PodMonitor over hand-maintained scrape_configs for rolling updates and stable DNS.

nodes.label: Recording rules
nodes.rationale: Precompute expensive PromQL once per evaluation_interval for dashboards and alerts instead of recomputing ad hoc.

nodes.label: Terraform
nodes.rationale: Describe infrastructure declaratively; computes dependency graphs and reconciles cloud APIs via providers.

nodes.label: Terraform state
nodes.rationale: Maps logical addresses to real IDs; must stay consistent with remote APIs.

nodes.label: terraform plan / apply
nodes.rationale: plan diffs desired config vs state + remote APIs; apply executes creates/updates/deletes through providers.

nodes.label: Terraform CLI lifecycle
nodes.rationale: Standard flow: init → validate → fmt → plan -out → apply plan file; plan without apply gates merges.

nodes.label: Terraform workspaces
nodes.rationale: CLI workspaces split state namespaces but are easy to misuse; many teams prefer separate env directories and backend keys.

nodes.label: Infrastructure drift
nodes.rationale: Default plans refresh remote objects to detect manual console edits; ignoring drift breeds state divergence.

nodes.label: lifecycle prevent_destroy
nodes.rationale: Guards critical datasets; combine with backups and prod approvals.

nodes.label: Remote backend (S3 + DynamoDB)
nodes.rationale: Remote state needs locking and backup discipline; S3 + DynamoDB locking is a common AWS pattern.

nodes.label: Terraform Cloud / Enterprise
nodes.rationale: Adds RBAC, speculative plans, policy sets; often replaces bespoke S3+Dynamo setups.

nodes.label: terraform_remote_state
nodes.rationale: Explicit output contracts beat implicit coupling across stacks.

nodes.label: Terraform CI gates
nodes.rationale: fmt -check, validate, plan on PR with reviewer acknowledgement before apply; optional tfsec/checkov/tflint.

nodes.label: Terraform vs Kubernetes day-two split
nodes.rationale: Terraform provisions clusters/add-ons; kubectl/Helm/GitOps handle frequent microservice churn unless intentionally unified via kubernetes_* resources.

nodes.label: Per-environment Terraform roots
nodes.rationale: Each env/<name> is its own Terraform root with separate state keys to limit blast radius and locking pain.

nodes.label: Helm / Argo CD / Flux day-two
nodes.rationale: Terraform owns VPC/EKS/IAM skeletons; Helm/Argo CD/Flux deploy daily microservices to avoid long Terraform plans.

nodes.label: Use managed free tiers
nodes.rationale: Your time is the scarce resource; plan upgrade paths and read ToS/limits.

nodes.label: Zero-cost MVP stack
nodes.rationale: Paid domain plus Cloudflare DNS, Vercel/Pages hosting, Supabase/Firebase, Resend/Brevo, GitHub, Sentry/UptimeRobot.

nodes.label: Do not run your own mail server
nodes.rationale: EC2+Postfix leads to spam/blacklists; use Resend/Brevo/SES and own SPF/DKIM/DMARC on your domain.
nodes.norm_label: do not run your own mail server

nodes.label: Transactional email
nodes.rationale: User-action triggered mail needing high deliverability; distinct from marketing campaigns.

nodes.label: Marketing email
nodes.rationale: Requires unsubscribe, list consent (GDPR/CAN-SPAM); isolate reputation from transactional mail.

nodes.label: SPF / DKIM / DMARC
nodes.rationale: Core DNS deliverability records; start DMARC at p=none then tighten to quarantine/reject.

nodes.label: Minimal MVP ops stack
nodes.rationale: Deploy on Vercel with Sentry errors, UptimeRobot health pings, Cloudflare Analytics, and GitHub Actions CI.

nodes.label: DevOps / CI/CD lifecycle
nodes.rationale: Build, test, deploy automated on every change; requires upfront pipeline investment.

nodes.label: API gateway
nodes.rationale: Single public entry for client traffic; routes requests and applies TLS, auth, rate limits, transforms, observability. Keep thin — business rules stay in services.

nodes.label: North-south traffic
nodes.rationale: Client → gateway → service; gateway is the external trust boundary. East-west uses mesh or cluster DNS.

nodes.label: API gateway request flow
nodes.rationale: TLS → route match → policies → forward upstream → optional response transform.

nodes.label: API versioning strategies
nodes.rationale: URL path versioning is obvious at gateway; header/query alternatives exist with trade-offs.

nodes.label: JWT authentication at gateway
nodes.rationale: Gateway validates signature/exp/iss/aud; services do authorization. Avoid double validation work.

nodes.label: Gateway authn vs service authz
nodes.rationale: Gateway authenticates identity; services authorize actions (RBAC, ownership).

nodes.label: Gateway rate limiting
nodes.rationale: Protects upstream from abuse and unfair usage; reject with 429 and Retry-After; Redis-backed for multi-instance counters.

nodes.label: Circuit breaker
nodes.rationale: Closed/open/half-open states fail fast on upstream error spikes to avoid retry storms.

nodes.label: CloudFront + API Gateway + Lambda
nodes.rationale: Path split: /assets/* to S3, /api/* to HTTP API → Lambda; HTTP API preferred for lower latency/cost.

nodes.label: API gateway operations
nodes.rationale: Watch 4xx/5xx/latency/429; align timeouts CDN→gateway→service; debug via access logs and direct-upstream compare.

nodes.label: CDN (Content Delivery Network)
nodes.rationale: Serves cached copies from edge PoPs for lower latency, origin offload, and spike resilience; primarily static assets and selected GET APIs.

nodes.label: Pull CDN
nodes.rationale: CDN fetches from origin on miss and caches with TTL; dominant model for modern web apps with object-storage origins.

nodes.label: CDN cache key
nodes.rationale: Misconfigured keys cause wrong cached content or low hit rate; exclude noise query params like utm_*.

nodes.label: Versioned / hashed asset URLs
nodes.rationale: Ship new hashed filenames with long immutable TTL so deploys need no CDN purge for JS/CSS.

nodes.label: private, no-store for authenticated APIs
nodes.rationale: Public caching of authenticated responses risks leaking user A's data to user B.

nodes.label: Origin Access Control (OAC)
nodes.rationale: Keeps S3 private so only CloudFront can fetch objects; avoid public buckets for private assets.

nodes.label: Multiple origins / path behaviors
nodes.rationale: Split by path on one distribution: long TTL for /assets/*, short/no cache for /api/*, short TTL for index.html.

nodes.label: Hashed static files
nodes.rationale: Content hashes in filenames make immutable long-TTL caching safe; only index.html needs frequent invalidation.

nodes.label: Versioned filenames over purge-all
nodes.rationale: Purging /* spikes origin and slows rollout; versioned filenames leave old JS cached but unused — preferred approach.

nodes.label: Service workers (PWA)
nodes.rationale: Can override CDN in the browser; misconfigured SW causes stale app despite CDN purge.

nodes.label: Cacheable public GET
nodes.rationale: Only when no user secrets, same URL→same bytes (or correct Vary), stale window acceptable, and explicit Cache-Control.

nodes.label: private no-store for personal APIs
nodes.rationale: Routes like /api/me, /cart, Set-Cookie responses, and mutating methods must not be CDN-cached.

nodes.label: Edge workers / compute at edge
nodes.rationale: Stateless lightweight logic at PoP (A/B, geo, token check) — not full database access.

nodes.label: Dynamic site acceleration
nodes.rationale: Routes dynamic traffic via optimized paths without caching body — distinct from caching.

nodes.label: Stale content playbook
nodes.rationale: Confirm symptom → curl hit/miss → check deploy → purge specific paths → verify Cache-Control; avoid habitual purge-all.

nodes.label: CDN security incident response
nodes.rationale: Remove at origin, purge CDN paths, rotate secrets if leaked, review cache key so auth responses were not cacheable.

nodes.label: API gateway
nodes.rationale: Routes and enforces policy on dynamic API traffic: auth, rate limit, service routing — not business rules.

nodes.label: CDN + API gateway topology
nodes.rationale: CDN caches static/public GETs; gateway handles /api/* with auth and rate limits; typical SaaS uses both.

nodes.label: Thin gateway pattern
nodes.rationale: Validate, route, limit — keep business rules out of gateway config.

nodes.label: Document model (BSON/JSON)
nodes.rationale: One read returns nested JSON fitting many REST shapes; flexible schema still needs planned migrations.

nodes.label: Polyglot persistence with Postgres
nodes.rationale: Use Postgres for money + invariants, MongoDB for flexible documents — many teams run both.

nodes.label: Embed vs reference
nodes.rationale: Embed what you fetch together and keep bounded; reference huge one-to-many, shared docs, or independently updated data.

nodes.label: Keyset (seek) pagination
nodes.rationale: Offset/skip slows at large offsets; keyset with _id or createdAt+_id matching the index is preferred.

nodes.label: MongoDB connection pooling
nodes.rationale: One MongoClient per process; tune maxPoolSize to avoid thousands of Atlas connections.

nodes.label: Sharding / shard key
nodes.rationale: Choose high-cardinality even keys; monotonic _id alone causes hot shards; hard to change later.

nodes.label: ESR compound index rule
nodes.rationale: Equality → Sort → Range fields in compound index order.

nodes.label: MongoDB optimization workflow
nodes.rationale: Measure slow ops → explain → fix access pattern → add/adjust index → re-measure; schema before scale.

nodes.label: PL/SQL in DB vs application code
nodes.rationale: Default for new services: keep most logic in the app; use PL/SQL where Oracle is system of record for batch/ETL and client-agnostic rules.
nodes.norm_label: pl/sql in db vs application code

nodes.label: %TYPE / %ROWTYPE anchors
nodes.rationale: Anchored variables follow column/row type changes after migrations — fewer silent mismatches.

nodes.label: Set-based SQL over row-by-row
nodes.rationale: Prefer single UPDATE/MERGE/INSERT…SELECT; cursor loops are anti-patterns when one statement suffices.

nodes.label: AUTHID DEFINER vs CURRENT_USER
nodes.rationale: DEFINER runs as owner (risk if over-privileged); CURRENT_USER respects invoker privileges — safer for multi-tenant.

nodes.label: PL/SQL package (spec + body)
nodes.rationale: Namespace, encapsulation, session state, and parse-tree reuse — avoid package globals with connection pools.

nodes.label: When not to use triggers
nodes.rationale: Hidden business rules, cross-table orchestration, and frequently changing logic belong in app services — triggers surprise ORMs.

nodes.label: PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION
nodes.rationale: Separate commit scope for audit even if outer rolls back — powerful and easy to misuse.

nodes.label: Bind variables / shared SQL
nodes.rationale: Literals in dynamic SQL cause hard parses and V$SQL churn; use binds via USING or PreparedStatement.

nodes.label: SQLite vs Postgres
nodes.rationale: SQLite for local tools/tests; Postgres when multiple app instances or users share one database.

nodes.label: Least-privilege app roles
nodes.rationale: App role gets SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on needed tables — not SUPERUSER.

nodes.label: Versioned schema migrations
nodes.rationale: Expand→deploy→contract; never rewrite applied history; fix forward with a new migration.

nodes.label: JSONB
nodes.rationale: Prefer JSONB over JSON for queryable document fields — binary and indexable with GIN.

nodes.label: B-tree index
nodes.rationale: Default Postgres index for equality, range, and sorting

nodes.label: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS)
nodes.rationale: Measure plans before adding indexes; focus on actual time and row estimates

nodes.label: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
nodes.rationale: Avoids long write locks but cannot run in a transaction; failed builds leave INVALID indexes

nodes.label: N+1 queries
nodes.rationale: Indexes alone do not fix N+1; fix in app with batch/JOIN

nodes.label: PreparedStatement
nodes.rationale: Parameter binding prevents SQL injection and enables plan cache reuse

nodes.label: HikariCP
nodes.rationale: Size pool from expected concurrent requests; Postgres max_connections is shared

nodes.label: Flyway
nodes.rationale: Flyway owns schema; use ddl-auto validate in production

nodes.label: Testcontainers
nodes.rationale: Prefer real Postgres over H2 for Postgres-specific features

nodes.label: Role separation
nodes.rationale: App uses least-privilege; postgres superuser is break-glass only

nodes.label: Point-in-time recovery
nodes.rationale: Needs base backup plus continuous WAL archive

nodes.label: PgBouncer
nodes.rationale: Multiplexes many app pools into fewer Postgres connections; transaction pooling may break session features

nodes.label: Optimization workflow
nodes.rationale: Measure first, one change at a time, verify with same workload

nodes.label: Keyset pagination
nodes.rationale: Beats large OFFSET; needs index on (created_at, id)

nodes.label: Table partitioning
nodes.rationale: Range partitions prune old data for time-series / large tables

nodes.label: Short transactions
nodes.rationale: Do not hold open while calling HTTP APIs; reduces locks and bloat

nodes.label: Git
nodes.rationale: Distributed VCS: full history locally; commit offline then sync

nodes.label: GitHub
nodes.rationale: Hosting + UI + PRs + Actions; not required for version control

nodes.label: ~/.ssh/config
nodes.rationale: Chooses which private key apps use for git@ remotes; IdentitiesOnly avoids wrong key

nodes.label: git rebase
nodes.rationale: Do not rebase commits already pushed and shared

nodes.label: --force-with-lease
nodes.rationale: Safer than --force; fails if remote moved unexpectedly

nodes.label: git revert
nodes.rationale: Safe for published history; creates forward undo commit

nodes.label: git commit --amend
nodes.rationale: Only amend commits not yet pushed or team-agreed

nodes.label: GitHub Flow
nodes.rationale: main always deployable; short-lived feature branches via PR

nodes.label: Rotate secrets first
nodes.rationale: History rewrite does not undo exposure; assume compromised if public

nodes.label: Branch protection
nodes.rationale: Require PR, status checks, and reviews before merging to main

nodes.label: Apache Kafka
nodes.rationale: Holds events; database holds current state — not for ad-hoc queries

nodes.label: KRaft
nodes.rationale: Modern Kafka metadata without ZooKeeper

nodes.label: Partition key
nodes.rationale: Same key routes to same partition for entity ordering

nodes.label: Transactional outbox
nodes.rationale: HTTP handler never kafka.send() inside same transaction as order insert

nodes.label: Stripe manual capture (hold)
nodes.rationale: Verify stock/rules before taking money; release hold instead of refunding

nodes.label: 202 Accepted + status poll
nodes.rationale: Payment/booking take seconds over Kafka; holding HTTP open is fragile

nodes.label: Kafka producer
nodes.rationale: Serializes records and sends batches; use acks=all and enable.idempotence for durable produce

nodes.label: Kafka consumer
nodes.rationale: Polls, deserializes, processes, then commits offsets; disable auto-commit in production

nodes.label: Idempotent producer
nodes.rationale: Retries must not create duplicate records at the broker

nodes.label: Schema Registry
nodes.rationale: Production event buses adopt schema registry once multiple teams publish

nodes.label: Producer ack
nodes.rationale: Broker confirms record stored per acks config

nodes.label: Consumer ack
nodes.rationale: Offset commit — restart skips processed records

nodes.label: acks=all
nodes.rationale: Strongest durability; production default for domain events; waits for ISR

nodes.label: At-least-once delivery
nodes.rationale: Commit after process; may duplicate — prefer with idempotent handler

nodes.label: enable.auto.commit
nodes.rationale: true risks at-most-once if commit before handler finishes

nodes.label: Consumer group
nodes.rationale: Each partition assigned to at most one consumer in the group

nodes.label: Idempotent consumer
nodes.rationale: Default: at-least-once + idempotent handler (natural key or event ids)

nodes.label: Multi-group fan-out
nodes.rationale: Different groups read every message independently with own offsets

nodes.label: Partition key design
nodes.rationale: Same key → same partition → ordered events; hot keys overload one partition

nodes.label: Transactional outbox
nodes.rationale: DB commit and Kafka send are two systems; write business row + outbox in one TX, relay publishes

nodes.label: Dead letter queue (DLQ/DLT)
nodes.rationale: Commit main offset after routing poison messages so partition is not blocked forever

nodes.label: Saga orchestrator
nodes.rationale: One service owns state machine; best for visibility and compensations

nodes.label: Choreographed chain
nodes.rationale: Each service publishes next event only on success — Kafka-native pipeline

nodes.label: Parallel fan-out vs sequential pipeline
nodes.rationale: Multiple groups on one topic = parallel; strict A→B→C needs pipeline topics or orchestrator

nodes.label: Bootstrap
nodes.rationale: CSS-first UI framework; JS optional for interactive widgets

nodes.label: Less
nodes.rationale: Legacy preprocessor popular with Bootstrap 3; prefer Sass for greenfield

nodes.label: Sass
nodes.rationale: Most common modern preprocessor; SCSS is default syntax

nodes.label: Sass @use modules
nodes.rationale: Prefer @use/@forward over deprecated @import

nodes.label: Flutter lib/ layout
nodes.rationale: screens compose UI; services talk to server; widgets draw passed-in data

nodes.label: ListView.builder
nodes.rationale: Virtualized lists — never build huge Column of children

nodes.label: Protected routes (go_router redirect)
nodes.rationale: Client auth state is UX only — server must validate every request

nodes.label: HTMX
nodes.rationale: Hypermedia: server returns HTML fragments; HTMX swaps into page

nodes.label: Hypermedia-driven app
nodes.rationale: Removes fetch/render boilerplate for CRUD while keeping server-rendered templates

nodes.label: 422 validation swap
nodes.rationale: Re-render form partial with errors — classic server-side validation without JSON DTOs

nodes.label: htmx:beforeSwap for 422
nodes.rationale: Allow swap on 422 so validation HTML still replaces the form

nodes.label: Partials + swap
nodes.rationale: Common UI patterns map cleanly to partials + swap

nodes.label: HTMX
nodes.rationale: Deliberate architectural choice: hypermedia on the server, minimal client state

nodes.label: HTMX MPA
nodes.rationale: Upgrade path from server-rendered apps, not a rewrite

nodes.label: Hybrid architecture
nodes.rationale: Marketing static/SSR, HTMX app shell, JSON API, React islands — do not force one paradigm

nodes.label: When not to use HTMX
nodes.rationale: Avoid for OT/CRDT, media editors, CAD, games, app-store mobile, API-only backend policy

nodes.label: Pass-by-value
nodes.rationale: Primitives copy bits; references copy the pointer — mutating object is visible, reassigning parameter is not

nodes.label: Composition over inheritance
nodes.rationale: Prefer composition over deep inheritance; deep inheritance creates brittle base-class contracts

nodes.label: PECS
nodes.rationale: Producer-extends, consumer-super for wildcards

nodes.label: Why automate tests
nodes.rationale: Regression safety, specification, refactoring courage

nodes.label: Test-driven debugging
nodes.rationale: When fixing a bug, add a failing test first when feasible

nodes.label: Array vs List
nodes.rationale: Fixed arrays for hot numeric buffers; ArrayList for most application collections

nodes.label: Virtual threads
nodes.rationale: Lightweight threads ideal for I/O-bound work without one platform thread per request

nodes.label: Auto-configuration
nodes.rationale: If Hibernate + datasource on classpath, JPA set-up appears unless opted out

nodes.label: Layered packages
nodes.rationale: Controllers depend on services; services on repositories — avoid controllers injecting repositories directly
