nodes.label: @ConfigurationProperties
nodes.rationale: Prefer grouped typed properties with validation over many unrelated @Value fields

nodes.label: Type-safe config choice
nodes.rationale: @Value for one-offs; @ConfigurationProperties for structured validated settings; Environment for dynamic keys

nodes.label: Constructor injection
nodes.rationale: Makes dependencies final and guarantees object usable after construction — prefer over field @Autowired

nodes.label: Request/response DTOs
nodes.rationale: Keep entities out of the wire format — use dedicated records

nodes.label: REQUIRES_NEW
nodes.rationale: Use sparingly for audit/side records that must persist even if outer tx rolls back

nodes.label: AOP proxy self-invocation pitfall
nodes.rationale: @Transactional only works on public methods called from outside the class via proxy

nodes.label: Transaction boundaries in services
nodes.rationale: Keep @Transactional on @Service methods not @RestController

nodes.label: Don't swallow exceptions
nodes.rationale: catch without rethrow may commit a transaction you thought failed

nodes.label: Cache-aside
nodes.rationale: App reads cache; on miss load DB, write cache; own invalidation via @CacheEvict, TTL, or messages

nodes.label: Saga pattern
nodes.rationale: Break workflow into local transactions plus compensating actions when a later step fails

nodes.label: Transactional outbox
nodes.rationale: Write business data and outbox row in same local tx; separate process publishes to broker

nodes.label: Two-phase commit / XA
nodes.rationale: Usually avoid — tight coupling, higher latency; last resort for regulation/legacy

nodes.label: Eventual consistency
nodes.rationale: Default in microservices: each service commits locally then notifies others

nodes.label: Virtual threads
nodes.rationale: Not reactive — controller still blocks; JVM parks virtual thread while waiting

nodes.label: Virtual thread pinning
nodes.rationale: Long synchronized blocks or native code pin virtual thread to carrier and reduce gains

nodes.label: MVC vs WebFlux choice
nodes.rationale: Default MVC + virtual threads; WebFlux when clear reactive requirement (streaming, backpressure, R2DBC)

nodes.label: HTMX
nodes.rationale: Forms, dashboards, mostly server HTML — HTMX + templates as alternative to SPA

nodes.label: Picking a JS approach
nodes.rationale: SPA → React/Angular; mobile → RN/Flutter; forms/dashboards → HTMX; small widget → vanilla/Bootstrap/Alpine

nodes.label: Angular
nodes.rationale: Full framework (Google) for SPAs with TypeScript, DI, and RxJS; batteries-included vs React library model.

nodes.label: Standalone Components
nodes.rationale: Modern default — no NgModule required per feature.

nodes.label: core/shared/features layout
nodes.rationale: components display; services fetch; core wires auth/HTTP once.

nodes.label: Lazy loadComponent
nodes.rationale: Keeps initial bundle smaller — one file per route.

nodes.label: OnPush Change Detection
nodes.rationale: Component checks only when inputs change or events fire — performance.

nodes.label: APP_INITIALIZER auth bootstrap
nodes.rationale: Avoid flash of login on protected routes by restoring session at start.

nodes.label: Reactive Forms
nodes.rationale: Enterprise apps standardize on reactive for testability and complex validation.

nodes.label: Bootstrap
nodes.rationale: CSS + vanilla JS plugins (BS5+); data-bs-* declarative markup; pairs with HTMX/server HTML.

nodes.label: jQuery
nodes.rationale: Legacy DOM/AJAX helper; poor default for new SPAs.

nodes.label: React
nodes.rationale: Component-based UI library from Meta; JSX functions of state; ecosystem covers routing/SSR.

nodes.label: pages/views folder pattern
nodes.rationale: Consistency matters more than label; pages/views/screens usually mean one component per route.

nodes.label: Feature slices pattern
nodes.rationale: Colocate domain under features/<name>/ when 5+ domains and multiple devs.

nodes.label: TanStack Query
nodes.rationale: Server-state cache keyed by queryKey; reduces stale-response bugs vs manual useEffect.

nodes.label: Stale response cancellation guard
nodes.rationale: Cleanup cancelled flag or abort so late responses do not overwrite newer UI state.

nodes.label: React Native
nodes.rationale: iOS/Android apps with React components rendered as native widgets; share skills/API layer with web React.

nodes.label: Expo
nodes.rationale: Toolkit/SDK/build services on top of RN — recommended start for SWE101.

nodes.label: expo prebuild
nodes.rationale: Middle ground: generate ios/android from app.json while keeping Expo SDK/EAS.

nodes.label: screens/ + api/ layout
nodes.rationale: screens compose UI; api talks to server; components draw props.

nodes.label: FlatList
nodes.rationale: Virtualizes long lists — prefer over map in ScrollView.

nodes.label: Mermaid
nodes.rationale: Text-to-diagram in Markdown; native GitHub render; prefer when viewer needs no build step.

nodes.label: Text-based diagrams
nodes.rationale: Diffable in PRs, portable across GitHub/Notion/MkDocs, fast to edit.

nodes.label: Pin mermaid-cli version
nodes.rationale: Lockfile + pinned version keeps CI and local renders aligned when layout changes.

nodes.label: Mermaid modular design without !include
nodes.rationale: Mermaid core has no !include; split by bounded context and prepend shared theme via build script

nodes.label: GitHub native Mermaid rendering
nodes.rationale: GitHub natively renders Mermaid in Markdown unlike PlantUML

nodes.label: Inline mermaid for GitHub-first teams
nodes.rationale: Recommended: inline ```mermaid in Markdown; add .mmd + SVG for slides/PDFs

nodes.label: Text-based diagrams
nodes.rationale: Diffable, repeatable, fast to edit, embeddable, composable via !include

nodes.label: PlantUML vs Mermaid rule of thumb
nodes.rationale: Use PlantUML for UML fidelity and includes; Mermaid when viewer renders with no build step
nodes.norm_label: plantuml vs mermaid rule of thumb

nodes.label: Avoid public PlantUML servers for proprietary designs
nodes.rationale: Public servers receive diagram source; use local JAR or self-hosted server
nodes.norm_label: avoid public plantuml servers for proprietary designs

nodes.label: Pin or vendor C4 includes
nodes.rationale: Remote !include URLs can change; pin to commit SHA or vendor into repo

nodes.label: When to use class vs activity vs state vs ER
nodes.rationale: Class for domain nouns; activity for workflows; state for lifecycles; ER for table relationships
nodes.norm_label: when to use class vs activity vs state vs er

nodes.label: GitHub does not natively render PlantUML
nodes.rationale: Unlike Mermaid; embed via SVG link or docs-site plugin
nodes.norm_label: github does not natively render plantuml

nodes.label: Avoid mutable default arguments
nodes.rationale: Defaults evaluated once at definition; use None + inner init

nodes.label: Inheritance vs composition
nodes.rationale: Prefer composition when behavior varies independently; inheritance encodes is-a

nodes.label: Always pass encoding=utf-8
nodes.rationale: Default locale encoding still bites on Windows; portable apps set utf-8 explicitly

nodes.label: ML reproducibility checklist
nodes.rationale: Fix seeds, pin deps, version datasets; avoid silent metric drift

nodes.label: MSVC Build Tools on Windows
nodes.rationale: Default Windows toolchain needs MSVC linker; cargo build fails without link.exe

nodes.label: Do not hold std::Mutex across await
nodes.rationale: Holding std::sync::Mutex while suspended causes deadlocks; drop guard or use tokio::sync::Mutex
nodes.norm_label: do not hold std::mutex across await

nodes.label: When not to use async
nodes.rationale: CLI one-shots, CPU-bound jobs, and simple scripts are often better sync

nodes.label: Rust vs Zig mental map
nodes.rationale: Zig uses explicit allocators and error sets; no borrow checker or language async today

nodes.label: No hidden heap / no GC
nodes.rationale: Caller provides Allocator; document who frees returned memory

nodes.label: Explicit if-routing trade-off
nodes.rationale: No annotation layer; every route is an explicit if in router.zig — more boilerplate, full control

nodes.label: TLS termination at reverse proxy
nodes.rationale: Recommended production pattern for Zig web apps: terminate TLS at nginx/Caddy/Traefik/ALB and keep std.http.Server on plain TCP to avoid TLS API churn and match industry practice.

nodes.label: Obsidian
nodes.rationale: Local-first Markdown vault app for personal knowledge bases, ADRs, runbooks, and project wikis without a hosted wiki.

nodes.label: Local-first Markdown notes
nodes.rationale: Plain files survive app changes, diff in Git, work offline; prefer Obsidian when you want your own Markdown corpus with links and plugins.

nodes.label: .obsidian Git conventions
nodes.rationale: Commit shared plugin/settings; gitignore workspace.json as machine-specific.

nodes.label: Tags vs folders
nodes.rationale: Folders give one primary location; tags are cross-cutting labels; avoid tag explosion with a controlled vocabulary.

nodes.label: One sync source of truth
nodes.rationale: Mixing Obsidian Sync and Git on the same vault causes pain; pick one source of truth.

nodes.label: Redis
nodes.rationale: In-memory store for low-latency cache, sessions, rate limits; usually not system of record — pair with Postgres/MongoDB.

nodes.label: Distributed lock
nodes.rationale: Use sparingly; prefer idempotent jobs and DB unique constraints; unlock only if value matches (Lua/token).

nodes.label: Cache-optional fallback
nodes.rationale: On Redis failure, fall back to database — slower but available when cache is optional.

nodes.label: Round-trip reduction
nodes.rationale: Network RTT dominates; use MGET/pipelines instead of sequential GET/SET loops.

nodes.label: Shared Redis sessions
nodes.rationale: One server-side session in Redis plus shared cookie Domain=.example.com lets multiple subdomain apps share login without sticky sessions.

nodes.label: SSO for different domains
nodes.rationale: Different registrable domains cannot share cookies; use OAuth/OIDC IdP; Redis holds IdP sessions or refresh/revocation, not a cross-domain cookie.

nodes.label: Redis session vs JWT
nodes.rationale: Redis sessions enable logout-everywhere and role revoke; JWT common for mobile; many teams use Redis session for web subdomains and JWT for mobile.

nodes.label: RAM working set formula
nodes.rationale: Use concurrent users × bytes per active user, not DAU; add 2× headroom before sizing nodes.

nodes.label: Bottleneck
nodes.rationale: The saturated resource caps throughput; relieving one moves the limit — systematic measurement beats guessing.

nodes.label: Chatty RPC
nodes.rationale: Many round trips multiply latency; fix with batch APIs and gRPC streaming.

nodes.label: Connection pool
nodes.rationale: Reuse TCP connections with keep-alive to avoid new conn per request.

nodes.label: HTTP/2 multiplexing
nodes.rationale: Fixes HTTP/1.1 head-of-line blocking with one connection and many streams.

nodes.label: CDN and edge caching
nodes.rationale: Offload static and cacheable API responses; reduce cross-region RTT.

nodes.label: Latency budget
nodes.rationale: Design APIs so critical path minimizes cross-region hops.

nodes.label: N+1 queries
nodes.rationale: ORM lazy load causes 1+N queries; fix with JOIN, batch IN, or DataLoader.

nodes.label: Connection pool exhaustion
nodes.rationale: Many pods × pool size can exceed DB max_connections.

nodes.label: PgBouncer / RDS Proxy
nodes.rationale: Multiplex many app connections to few DB connections.

nodes.label: Read replicas
nodes.rationale: For read-heavy workloads that tolerate lag.

nodes.label: Database sharding
nodes.rationale: When write scale exceeds single primary.

nodes.label: CQRS
nodes.rationale: Async queue and CQRS help when single primary write ceiling is hit.

nodes.label: Thundering herd
nodes.rationale: Cache TTL expiry causes concurrent misses that all hit the DB.

nodes.label: Request coalescing / singleflight
nodes.rationale: One refill of cache; others wait.

nodes.label: Circuit breaker
nodes.rationale: Stop calling failing dependency; closed → open → half-open.

nodes.label: Bulkhead
nodes.rationale: Isolate pool per dependency.

nodes.label: Five-phase bottleneck process
nodes.rationale: Measure first, fix in impact order, validate, prevent recurrence.

nodes.label: Fix by impact / cost
nodes.rationale: Prefer query/index and cache before sharding or architecture rewrite.

nodes.label: Interview design flow
nodes.rationale: Requirements → estimate QPS/storage → high-level diagram → deep dive hot path → bottlenecks.

nodes.label: Shared building blocks
nodes.rationale: Cache, queue, CDN, search index, sharded DB recur across classic designs.

nodes.label: Hash + truncate
nodes.rationale: Stateless but collisions must be checked in DB.

nodes.label: Key Generation Service (KGS)
nodes.rationale: Pre-allocates batches; no collision at write; extra service.

nodes.label: 301 vs 302 redirect
nodes.rationale: 302 for accurate analytics; 301 caches and under-counts clicks.

nodes.label: Fan-out on write (push)
nodes.rationale: Write post id to every follower's feed cache; O(1) read.

nodes.label: Fan-out on read (pull)
nodes.rationale: Write-cheap; merge N followees' posts on read.

nodes.label: Hybrid fan-out
nodes.rationale: Push for most users; pull for celebrities to avoid write amplification.

nodes.label: Celebrity follower threshold
nodes.rationale: If followers > X (e.g. 10K), skip push fan-out.

nodes.label: WebSocket transport
nodes.rationale: Low-latency persistent connection; chat default.

nodes.label: Presence
nodes.rationale: Redis key with TTL renewed by heartbeat.

nodes.label: Snowflake ID
nodes.rationale: Time-ordered unique IDs without central DB sequence.

nodes.label: Adaptive bitrate (ABR)
nodes.rationale: Player picks segment quality from measured bandwidth.

nodes.label: Redis Geo index
nodes.rationale: GEOADD/GEORADIUS for live matching.

nodes.label: Geohash
nodes.rationale: Edge cells need neighbor lookup.

nodes.label: Crawl politeness
nodes.rationale: robots.txt, crawl-delay, per-domain token bucket.

nodes.label: Bloom filter URL dedup
nodes.rationale: Tiny memory; false positives possible; no false negatives.

nodes.label: Consistent hash on domain
nodes.rationale: One worker owns a domain for centralized rate limits.

nodes.label: Checkout pattern progression
nodes.rationale: Local ACID → Outbox → Choreography/Saga → Sharded saga → Workflow engine; idempotency everywhere.

nodes.label: Checkout orchestrator
nodes.rationale: Dedicated service runs saga state machine; only component that knows full workflow.

nodes.label: Compensating transactions
nodes.rationale: On inventory failure after payment: refund then cancel order.

nodes.label: Eventual consistency across services
nodes.rationale: Customer sees CONFIRMED only after all forward steps succeed.

nodes.label: Event-driven coordination
nodes.rationale: Each service reacts to bus events; no central orchestrator.

nodes.label: Compensating events
nodes.rationale: InventoryFailed → Payment refunds → Order cancels.

nodes.label: Event schema contracts
nodes.rationale: Compensation order encoded in who listens; document contracts to avoid bugs.

nodes.label: Modular monolith
nodes.rationale: One app, one Postgres, one @Transactional COMMIT.

nodes.label: External payment outside DB txn
nodes.rationale: Stripe is not in Postgres transaction; use auth-then-capture or refund job.

nodes.label: Dual-write problem
nodes.rationale: Separate DB write and kafka.send can leave order without event or charge without order.

nodes.label: Outbox relay
nodes.rationale: Publishes unpublished outbox rows to Kafka; at-least-once.

nodes.label: Idempotency-Key
nodes.rationale: Client key stored to return cached response on retry; prevents double charge.

nodes.label: Layered idempotency
nodes.rationale: API, Payment service, Stripe, and event consumers each dedupe.

nodes.label: OpenSearch read model
nodes.rationale: Eventual lag acceptable; search index is not OLTP source of truth.

nodes.label: customer_id shard key
nodes.rationale: Order/Payment by customer_id so customer order history hits one shard.

nodes.label: sku shard key for inventory
nodes.rationale: Stock row lives with SKU; write spread across shards.

nodes.label: Temporal / Cadence
nodes.rationale: Engine stores saga state, retries, timers; workers call microservices.

nodes.label: Durable timers and crash recovery
nodes.rationale: Prefer engine when long-running checkout needs timers without custom cron.

nodes.label: REST
nodes.rationale: Models resources as URLs with HTTP verbs; servers stay stateless so session state lives in tokens or client storage.

nodes.label: gRPC
nodes.rationale: Binary Protocol Buffers over HTTP/2 with typed contracts and native streaming; best for internal service mesh.

nodes.label: GraphQL
nodes.rationale: Client describes exact response shape in one query; avoids over-fetching but risks N+1 and harder caching than REST URLs.

nodes.label: Cursor pagination
nodes.rationale: Stable under live feeds via opaque cursor on sort key; harder to jump to arbitrary pages than offset.

nodes.label: Offset pagination
nodes.rationale: Simple SQL OFFSET but can skip or duplicate rows when inserts/deletes happen while paging.

nodes.label: DataLoader
nodes.rationale: Batches GraphQL resolver lookups (e.g. userIds) into one SELECT WHERE id IN (...) to fix N+1.

nodes.label: Idempotency-Key
nodes.rationale: Header on POST (e.g. payments) enabling safe retries without duplicate side effects.

nodes.label: API versioning
nodes.rationale: Never break existing clients — add fields, deprecate, then sunset; strategies include URL path, header, or query.

nodes.label: Async messaging
nodes.rationale: Decouples producers from consumers in time and scale when peak traffic exceeds synchronous capacity.

nodes.label: Kafka
nodes.rationale: Ordered partition log with replay; order guaranteed per partition via partition key.

nodes.label: Dead-letter queue
nodes.rationale: Failed messages after N retries go to DLQ with alert so poison messages do not block the whole queue.

nodes.label: At-least-once delivery
nodes.rationale: May duplicate messages; production systems pair this with idempotent consumers and idempotency keys.

nodes.label: Transactional outbox
nodes.rationale: Write business row and outbox row in one DB transaction; relay publishes to broker so event is published iff business commit succeeded.

nodes.label: Rate limiting
nodes.rationale: Caps requests per client/window for abuse prevention, fairness, cost control, and stability; returns HTTP 429.

nodes.label: Token bucket
nodes.rationale: Bucket holds at most B tokens refilled at R/sec; allows bounded bursts then steady throughput.

nodes.label: Leaky bucket
nodes.rationale: Processes requests at fixed rate from a queue; smooths bursts with no large spikes.

nodes.label: Fixed window counter
nodes.rationale: Counts per calendar window; flawed because 2× burst can occur at window edges.

nodes.label: Sliding window counter
nodes.rationale: Blends previous and current window counts; good accuracy/memory balance, common in Redis.

nodes.label: Redis shared counter
nodes.rationale: Multiple API instances need a shared counter via Redis INCR+EXPIRE or sliding-window Lua.

nodes.label: Database sharding
nodes.rationale: Horizontal partitioning across independent DB instances for write and storage scale; exhaust replicas/cache/tuning first.

nodes.label: Replication
nodes.rationale: Copies same data to multiple nodes for read scale and HA; distinct from sharding's disjoint row sets.

nodes.label: Partitioning
nodes.rationale: Splits rows in one DB by range/list/hash to prune scans; still one primary unlike sharding.

nodes.label: Shard router
nodes.rationale: Picks physical shard from shard key; styles include app-level hash, Vitess/Citus proxy, ORM, or managed services.

nodes.label: Shard key
nodes.rationale: Most important design choice; needs high cardinality, access-aligned and write-aligned properties; co-locate entities queried together.

nodes.label: Consistent hashing
nodes.rationale: Maps keys and nodes on a ring so adding/removing a node moves only a slice of keys; used in DynamoDB, Cassandra, Redis Cluster.

nodes.label: Scatter-gather
nodes.rationale: Queries without shard key hit all shards and merge; fix with GSI, lookup table, denormalize, or require tenant/user id in API.

nodes.label: Hot shard
nodes.rationale: Skew from celebrities, tenant whales, or time hotspots; mitigate with sub-shard split, cache, write queue, or separate KV store.

nodes.label: Hash-based sharding
nodes.rationale: shard_id = hash(key) % N for even spread; no cheap range queries; changing N remaps many keys unless consistent hashing.

nodes.label: Range-based sharding
nodes.rationale: Range scans hit one shard but risk hot shard if new keys cluster on latest range.

nodes.label: Inverted index
nodes.rationale: Maps each token to posting list of (document_id, positions); beats LIKE scans for relevance ranking with BM25.

nodes.label: CDC
nodes.rationale: DB binlog/WAL via Debezium → Kafka → indexer; industry standard for keeping search in sync with eventual lag.

nodes.label: Dual-write to search
nodes.rationale: App writes DB and Elasticsearch in parallel; partial failure causes index drift.

nodes.label: Vector semantic search
nodes.rationale: Embed documents and queries; ANN search (HNSW, IVF); hybrid with BM25 via RRF fusion.

nodes.label: CDN
nodes.rationale: Caches content at edge PoPs near users for lower latency and less origin load.

nodes.label: Cache-Control TTL
nodes.rationale: Default caching via max-age; pair with versioned URLs for immutable assets and purge API for urgent invalidation.

nodes.label: Stale-while-revalidate
nodes.rationale: Serve stale content while fetching fresh to smooth traffic spikes.

nodes.label: Pull CDN
nodes.rationale: CDN fetches from origin on miss; suited to web apps and APIs with cache headers.

nodes.label: Two-phase commit
nodes.rationale: Prepare then commit for strong atomicity; blocking if coordinator dies; poor default across microservices.

nodes.label: Saga pattern
nodes.rationale: Sequence of local transactions with compensating steps on failure; preferred for microservices long-running flows with outbox.

nodes.label: Saga choreography
nodes.rationale: Each service reacts to events; no single coordinator SPOF but harder to see global state.

nodes.label: Saga orchestration
nodes.rationale: Central orchestrator sends commands; clear state machine and easier debug; orchestrator availability matters.

nodes.label: Idempotency keys
nodes.rationale: Client sends Idempotency-Key UUID; service maps key to response so at-least-once retries do not double-charge.

nodes.label: Distributed traces
nodes.rationale: Spans with W3C Trace Context headers show which hop was slow; correlate with metrics and logs via trace_id.

nodes.label: SLO and error budget
nodes.rationale: Alert on symptom burn rate (e.g. 99.9% availability ≈ 43 min/month budget) rather than noisy causes like CPU.

nodes.label: Chaos engineering
nodes.rationale: Deliberately inject failure in staging/controlled prod to validate restarts, timeouts, circuit breakers, and failover.

nodes.label: Runbook
nodes.rationale: Every alert should link a runbook covering symptom, dashboards, causes, mitigation, and escalation.
